body_txt: A spoiler is information about a story that is revealed to the audience before they experience it in the media directly. If someone learns beforehand about a major plot twist or character death, the surprise was spoiled. Because of how this can reduce the viewer's enjoyment of the story permanently, intentionally spreading spoilers is considered to be a seriously rude act in most communities. Which details of a story would be considered spoilers can vary widely between fandoms and individuals. Many online communities have measures in place to protect new members from spoilers, eg. a forum for a show that has a discussion thread for each episode could have a rule forbidding mentioning events in future episodes. Common kinds of spoilers, and how to avoid them: Subtypes of the media type of spoiler: Happens more for main networks. When a spoiler becomes common knowledge in the mainstream — such as Darth Vader turning out to be Luke Skywalker's father — it's no longer spoiler fodder and gets demoted to It Was His Sled . When a sequel to a work spoils its predecessors, it's a Late-Arrival Spoiler . Not to be confused with a spoiler that happens early or late in the work . But, on one hand, spoilers aren't always a bad thing. They can create sense of positive Dramatic Irony , for suspense. On the other hand, posting spoilers on a fan forum will almost always get the forumgoers royally pissed off at you, but when the show's actual owners start using or threatening legal action against people who spoil, you should Spoil at Your Own Risk . Not to be confused with the DC Comics character Spoiler (though there's a reason she's called that; see below). Or with Double Spoiler ~ Touhou Bunkachou , for that matter. Or with aircraft spoilers . For a few tips on how we treat spoilers around this wiki, see Handling Spoilers . See Spoilered Rotten for tropes about spoilers, and tropes where spoilers are almost inherent.
